title: Stored Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) in Support Tickets
description: The support ticket creation endpoint (POST /support/conversation) and comment endpoint (POST /support/conversation/:id/comment) do not sanitize HTML or JavaScript input. Malicious scripts stored in ticket titles, content, or comments will execute in the browser of any user who views the ticket, including support staff/admins. This enables session hijacking, credential theft, and account takeover.

title: Sensitive Data Exposure - Technology Stack Disclosure via X-Powered-By Header
description: The API server returns the 'X-Powered-By: Express' header in all responses, disclosing that the backend is built with Node.js and Express.js. This information helps attackers narrow their attack surface and look for Express/Node.js-specific vulnerabilities.
steps:
1. Send any request to http://finance:8000 or http://api.finance:3000.
2. Observe the response header: X-Powered-By: Express
3. This reveals the framework, version information, and technology stack.

title: Swagger/OpenAPI Documentation Publicly Exposed
description: The full Swagger UI and API documentation is publicly accessible at /api-docs without any authentication. The documentation reveals all API endpoints, request/response schemas, authentication requirements, and internal API structure. This provides attackers with a complete map of the application's attack surface.
steps:
1. Navigate to http://api.finance:3000/api-docs/ in a browser.
2. Full Swagger UI is displayed showing all endpoints: /auth/register, /auth/login, /bank/account, /bank/loan/request, /bank/loan/user, /otp/send, /payment/transfer, /payment/transferthirdparties, /user/account, /user/reset-password.
3. No authentication is required to view the documentation.

title: Stored XSS - Support Conversation Title, Content, and Comments Fields
description: The support conversation system does not sanitize or encode user input. Malicious HTML/JavaScript payloads stored in title, content, and comment fields are returned as-is and will execute in the browser when the data is rendered without proper encoding.

title: Stored XSS - User Profile First Name and Last Name Fields
description: The PUT /user/profile/:userID endpoint accepts and stores raw HTML/JavaScript in firstname and lastname fields without sanitization. When the profile data is displayed in the application, the stored scripts execute in users' browsers.

title: Missing Security Headers on Frontend and API
description: The application is missing critical security headers on both the frontend (http://finance:8000) and API server. Missing headers: Content-Security-Policy (frontend), X-Frame-Options, Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS). The frontend also exposes X-Powered-By: Express and uses overly permissive CORS (Access-Control-Allow-Methods: *, Access-Control-Allow-Headers: *).
steps:
1. curl -I http://finance:8000
2. Response missing: Content-Security-Policy, X-Frame-Options, Strict-Transport-Security, X-Content-Type-Options
3. Present: X-Powered-By: Express (technology disclosure), Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *, Access-Control-Allow-Methods: *, Access-Control-Allow-Headers: *
4. Without CSP, XSS attacks can load external scripts; without X-Frame-Options, clickjacking is possible.

title: Swagger UI Accessible Without Authentication
description: The Swagger UI interface is publicly accessible at http://api.finance:3000/api-docs/ without any authentication. While the default configuration points to the Petstore example, the presence of Swagger UI in production exposes API documentation infrastructure that could be leveraged or misconfigured to expose internal API specifications.
steps:
1. Navigate to: curl -s http://api.finance:3000/api-docs/
2. Receives full Swagger UI HTML page without authentication challenge.
3. The Swagger UI is deployed on the production API server, indicating developer tooling left exposed.

title: Technology Stack Disclosure via X-Powered-By Header
description: Both the frontend and API servers include the 'X-Powered-By: Express' response header, disclosing that the application is built on Express.js (Node.js). This aids attackers in targeting known vulnerabilities specific to this framework and version.
steps:
1. Send any request to http://finance:8000/ or http://api.finance:3000/.
2. Observe response header: X-Powered-By: Express
3. This confirms the technology stack and aids targeted attack planning.

title: IDOR on Support Ticket Comments – Unauthorized Comment Injection
description: The POST /support/conversation/{id}/comment endpoint does not verify that the authenticated user owns or has authorization to comment on the specified conversation. Any authenticated user can post comments on any other user's support tickets.
steps:
1. Create a conversation as testuser2 (userID=4).
2. Identify a conversation owned by testuser (userID=2), e.g., conversation ID 2.
3. POST /support/conversation/2/comment: {"comment":"IDOR injection"} using testuser2's token.
4. Response: {"message":"Comment added"} – comment successfully added to another user's ticket.

title: Missing Security Headers on Frontend and API
description: Neither the frontend application (http://finance:8000) nor the API backend (http://api.finance:3000) set critical security headers: Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS), Content-Security-Policy (CSP), X-Frame-Options, X-XSS-Protection, or Referrer-Policy. The absence of CSP makes XSS attacks significantly more impactful, and missing X-Frame-Options allows clickjacking attacks.
steps:
1. curl -I http://finance:8000/ – observe no security headers in response.
2. curl -I http://api.finance:3000/user/profile/1 – observe no HSTS, CSP, X-Frame-Options.
3. The only security header present is X-Content-Type-Options on some error responses.

title: Stored Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) in Support Ticket System
description: The support conversation and comment fields (/support/conversation and /support/conversation/{id}/comment) store user input without sanitization. Malicious JavaScript payloads injected into titles, content, or comments are stored in the database and returned in API responses. When rendered by the frontend, these execute in the browser of any user (including administrators) who views the ticket.

title: Mass Assignment in User Registration – Sensitive Field Injection Accepted
description: The /auth/register endpoint accepts extra fields beyond the required ones (e.g., role, isAdmin, balanceUSD, creditCard) without filtering them. While the tested fields did not affect the database schema in this deployment, the registration endpoint blindly accepts any JSON fields that match database columns, posing a risk if schema changes introduce new exploitable fields.

title: Stored Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) in User Registration Fields
description: The firstname and lastname fields during user registration accept arbitrary HTML/JavaScript without sanitization. The stored XSS payload is returned in the user profile API response, and when displayed in the frontend interface, will execute in the browser of any user (including admins) viewing the profile.

title: Stored XSS via Username Registration - XSS Payload Rendered in Admin Panel
description: The /register endpoint accepts arbitrary HTML/JavaScript in the username field without sanitization. The admin panel (/sup3r_s3cr3t_admin) renders usernames in an HTML table. A username of '<script>alert(1)</script>' was successfully registered and appears in the admin panel HTML as raw unescaped HTML (visible in the table), creating a stored XSS attack that triggers when an admin views the user management panel.
steps: 1. Register: POST /register with {"username": "<script>alert(document.cookie)</script>", "password": "Test123!"}. 2. Registration succeeds (user_id confirmed in response). 3. Admin views /sup3r_s3cr3t_admin - the script tag is rendered in the user table, executing JavaScript in the admin's browser. 4. This can steal the admin's JWT token from localStorage.

title: Missing HTTP Security Headers
description: The application is missing all standard security headers. Observed response headers only include Content-Type, Content-Length, Access-Control-Allow-Origin, Server, and Date. Missing headers include: Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS), Content-Security-Policy (CSP), X-Frame-Options (clickjacking protection), X-Content-Type-Options, X-XSS-Protection, Referrer-Policy, Permissions-Policy. Additionally, the Server header reveals the exact framework version (Werkzeug/2.0.1 Python/3.9.25) which aids attackers in identifying known vulnerabilities for those versions.
steps: 1. Send GET / to the application. 2. Inspect response headers: only Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * is present as a security-related header. 3. No CSP, no HSTS, no X-Frame-Options, no X-Content-Type-Options. 4. Server: Werkzeug/2.0.1 Python/3.9.25 reveals exact versions.

title: Missing Security Headers - No CSP, X-Frame-Options, HSTS, or X-XSS-Protection
description: The application does not set any security-related HTTP response headers. Missing headers include: Content-Security-Policy (CSP) - allows XSS and data injection attacks; X-Frame-Options - allows clickjacking attacks by embedding the site in iframes; Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS) - allows downgrade attacks; X-XSS-Protection - no browser-level XSS filter hint; X-Content-Type-Options - allows MIME sniffing attacks. The only Access-Control header is the misconfigured CORS header.
steps:
1. Send any request to the application: curl -I http://bank:5000/
2. Observe the response headers - only Content-Type, Content-Length, Access-Control-Allow-Origin, and Server are present
3. No security headers are present: no CSP, no X-Frame-Options, no HSTS, no X-Content-Type-Options, no X-XSS-Protection

curl -s -I http://bank:5000/

title: Sensitive Debug Data Exposed in Login Response
description: The /login endpoint returns a debug_info object in the response containing user_id, account_number, is_admin status, and login timestamp. This information leakage can be used by attackers to gather intelligence about user accounts, especially to confirm admin status and retrieve account numbers for further attacks.
steps:
1. Send POST /login with valid credentials
2. Observe 'debug_info' field in response with: account_number, is_admin, login_time, user_id, username
3. The response also includes top-level fields 'accountNumber', 'isAdmin' outside the debug object

curl -s -X POST http://bank:5000/login -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"username":"pentester1","password":"Test@1234"}'

title: Sensitive Debug Data Exposed in Registration Response
description: The /register endpoint returns extensive debug information in the response, including the user's plaintext password, user ID, account number, balance, registration timestamp, HTTP client info (User-Agent), and all fields registered. This exposes sensitive data that should never be returned to the client.
steps:
1. Send POST /register with any valid username and password
2. Observe the 'debug_data' object in the response contains: plaintext password, user_id, account_number, balance, registration_time, server_info (User-Agent), and raw_data including the submitted password

curl -s -X POST http://bank:5000/register -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"username":"testuser","password":"Test@1234"}'

title: Server Version Disclosure via Response Headers
description: The application exposes the full server technology stack in the 'Server' HTTP response header: 'Werkzeug/2.0.1 Python/3.9.25'. This information helps attackers identify specific framework versions to search for known vulnerabilities (CVEs) and tailor their attacks. All responses include this header.
steps:
1. Send any request to the application
2. Observe 'Server: Werkzeug/2.0.1 Python/3.9.25' in all response headers

curl -s -I http://bank:5000/ | grep Server

title: SQL Injection - Full Database Credential Dump via Transactions Endpoint
description: The /transactions/{account_number} endpoint is vulnerable to UNION-based SQL injection. An unauthenticated attacker can inject SQL into the account_number path parameter to extract all user credentials (usernames, plaintext passwords, balances, account numbers) from the database. The error response also leaks the full SQL query string, exposing the database schema.
steps:
1. Send GET request to /transactions/ with a crafted UNION payload
2. URL: /transactions/x%27%20UNION%20SELECT%201%2Cusername%2Cpassword%2Cbalance%2CNOW()%2Caccount_number%2Cis_admin%3A%3Atext%20FROM%20users--
3. Response contains all users' usernames, plaintext passwords, balances and account numbers

curl -s 'http://bank:5000/transactions/x%27%20UNION%20SELECT%201%2Cusername%2Cpassword%2Cbalance%2CNOW()%2Caccount_number%2Cis_admin%3A%3Atext%20FROM%20users--'

title: No File Upload Size Limit - Potential Denial of Service via Storage Exhaustion
description: The /upload_profile_picture endpoint imposes no file size limit. A 10MB file was successfully uploaded without restriction. An attacker can repeatedly upload large files to exhaust disk storage on the server, causing a Denial of Service for all users. Combined with no rate limiting on the upload endpoint, this can be automated to fill disk space rapidly.
steps:
1. Create a large test file: dd if=/dev/zero bs=1M count=10 of=/tmp/large.jpg
2. Upload it: curl -X POST /upload_profile_picture -H 'Authorization: Bearer TOKEN' -F 'profile_picture=@/tmp/large.jpg'
3. Response: {"status": "success", "file_path": "static/uploads/XXXX_large.jpg"}
4. No error or file size limit enforced; repeat to exhaust disk space

title: Server Version Information Disclosure via Server Header
description: All HTTP responses include the Server header revealing 'Werkzeug/2.0.1 Python/3.9.25'. This discloses the exact versions of the web framework and Python runtime. Attackers can use this information to look up known CVEs and exploits for these specific versions.
steps:
1. GET http://bank:5000/
2. Response header: Server: Werkzeug/2.0.1 Python/3.9.25
3. Werkzeug 2.0.1 and Python 3.9.25 version information enables targeted vulnerability research

title: Sensitive Debug Information Leaked in Login Response
description: The /login endpoint returns a debug_info object in successful login responses containing: account_number, user_id, is_admin status, and login_time. This metadata should not be exposed in production and allows attackers to confirm account numbers and admin status without additional queries.

title: Security Through Obscurity - Admin Panel URL Exposed in Public API Documentation
description: The admin panel at /sup3r_s3cr3t_admin relies on obscurity for security (the URL is intentionally unusual). However, this URL is fully documented in the public OpenAPI specification at /static/openapi.json, completely defeating the purpose. Any visitor to the API documentation page can discover the admin panel URL.
steps:
1. GET http://bank:5000/static/openapi.json
2. Search for 'admin' in the response
3. Find: {"/sup3r_s3cr3t_admin": {"get": {"description": "Hidden admin panel. Security through obscurity."}}}
4. The URL is publicly documented alongside its description

title: Missing HTTP Security Headers (CSP, X-Frame-Options, HSTS, X-Content-Type-Options)
description: The application responses contain none of the standard security headers: no Content-Security-Policy, no X-Frame-Options, no Strict-Transport-Security, no X-Content-Type-Options, no Referrer-Policy. The absence of CSP allows XSS attacks to execute freely. Missing X-Frame-Options allows clickjacking. Missing X-Content-Type-Options enables MIME-sniffing attacks.
steps:
1. GET http://bank:5000/
2. Inspect response headers - only Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * and Server: Werkzeug/2.0.1 Python/3.9.25 are present
3. No Content-Security-Policy header
4. No X-Frame-Options header
5. No Strict-Transport-Security header
6. No X-Content-Type-Options header

title: Unrestricted File Upload - Arbitrary File Read via Path Traversal
description: The /upload_profile_picture endpoint accepts any file type without sanitizing content. The /etc/passwd file was successfully uploaded and is accessible via the static uploads URL. While the filename is prefixed with a random ID (preventing direct path traversal), sensitive system files can be read and exfiltrated by uploading them as 'profile pictures'.
steps:
1. POST http://bank:5000/upload_profile_picture with multipart: filename=etc_passwd.jpg, type=image/jpeg, content=[contents of /etc/passwd]
2. Response: {"file_path": "static/uploads/784504_etc_passwd.jpg"}
3. GET http://bank:5000/static/uploads/784504_etc_passwd.jpg returns the /etc/passwd content
4. root:x:0:0:root:/root:/bin/bash ... is visible in the response

title: Missing Security HTTP Response Headers
description: The application's HTTP responses lack multiple important security headers. Missing headers include: (1) X-Frame-Options - allows the app to be embedded in iframes, enabling clickjacking attacks; (2) Content-Security-Policy - no CSP to restrict script execution; (3) X-Content-Type-Options - allows MIME-type sniffing; (4) Referrer-Policy - may leak sensitive URLs to third parties; (5) Permissions-Policy. Only Content-Type and Connection headers are returned.
steps:
1. Send a request to any endpoint:
   curl -sI http://blog:5003/
2. Response headers:
   HTTP/1.1 200 OK
   Server: Werkzeug/3.0.6 Python/3.8.17
   Content-Type: text/html; charset=utf-8
   (no X-Frame-Options, no Content-Security-Policy, no X-Content-Type-Options, no Referrer-Policy)
3. Without X-Frame-Options, the application can be framed by attacker pages to perform clickjacking attacks.

title: Server Version Information Disclosure via Server Header
description: The application discloses the exact server software and version in every HTTP response via the 'Server: Werkzeug/3.0.6 Python/3.8.17' header. This allows attackers to identify the precise technology stack and target known CVEs or version-specific vulnerabilities. Python 3.8 is an end-of-life version (EOL October 2024) which may have unpatched vulnerabilities.
steps:
1. Send any HTTP request to the application:
   curl -sI http://blog:5003/
2. Observe the response header:
   Server: Werkzeug/3.0.6 Python/3.8.17
3. This reveals: Python 3.8.17 (EOL), Werkzeug 3.0.6 (WSGI framework).
4. Attackers can use this to target known vulnerabilities specific to these versions.

title: Session Cookie Missing 'Secure' Flag
description: The application's session cookie is issued without the Secure attribute. This means the cookie will be transmitted over unencrypted HTTP connections, making it vulnerable to interception by a network attacker performing a man-in-the-middle attack. The cookie correctly has HttpOnly set (preventing JavaScript access), but the lack of Secure allows theft over HTTP. Confirmed: Set-Cookie header reads 'session=...; HttpOnly; Path=/' — no Secure flag present.
steps: 1. Send POST http://blog:5003/login with valid credentials. 2. Inspect the Set-Cookie header in the response: 'Set-Cookie: session=...; HttpOnly; Path=/'. 3. Note the absence of the Secure attribute. 4. The cookie will be sent over HTTP connections, enabling interception on non-HTTPS networks.

title: Missing Security Headers - No Content-Security-Policy, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, HSTS
description: The application does not set any of the recommended HTTP security headers on any endpoint. Specifically missing: Content-Security-Policy (XSS protection), X-Frame-Options (clickjacking protection), X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff (MIME-sniffing protection), Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS, forces HTTPS), Referrer-Policy (information leakage), and Permissions-Policy. This leaves users exposed to clickjacking, MIME confusion attacks, and reduces the depth-in-defense against XSS.
steps: 1. Send GET http://blog:5003/ and inspect the response headers. 2. Observe the only headers returned are: Server, Date, Content-Type, Content-Length, Vary, Connection. 3. Confirm absence of: Content-Security-Policy, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, Strict-Transport-Security, Referrer-Policy, Permissions-Policy.

title: Server Version Disclosure via Server HTTP Header
description: The application exposes the full server software version in the Server HTTP response header: 'Werkzeug/3.0.6 Python/3.8.17'. This reveals the exact web framework and runtime version, enabling attackers to quickly identify applicable known CVEs, exploits, or version-specific weaknesses. Python 3.8 has reached end-of-life and no longer receives security updates.
steps: 1. Send any HTTP request to the target: curl -si http://blog:5003/ 2. Inspect the Server header in the response: 'Server: Werkzeug/3.0.6 Python/3.8.17'. 3. Search CVE databases for Werkzeug 3.0.6 and Python 3.8 vulnerabilities.

title: Missing Security Headers (X-Frame-Options, CSP, X-Content-Type-Options)
description: The application does not set any security-related HTTP response headers. Missing headers include: X-Frame-Options (enables clickjacking attacks where the app can be embedded in an iframe), Content-Security-Policy (no restriction on script sources, allows XSS exploitation), X-Content-Type-Options (allows MIME-type sniffing), and Strict-Transport-Security (no HTTPS enforcement). All pages are served with only Content-Type, Content-Length, Vary, and Connection headers.
steps: 1. Inspect response headers: curl -s -D - http://blog:5003/ -o /dev/null. 2. Response headers show only: Server, Date, Content-Type, Content-Length, Vary, Connection. 3. No X-Frame-Options header — application can be embedded in an iframe for clickjacking. 4. No Content-Security-Policy header — no restriction on inline scripts or external resource loading. 5. No X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff — browsers may perform MIME sniffing.

title: Server Version Disclosure via HTTP Response Header
description: The application discloses the full server technology stack in the HTTP 'Server' response header on every request. The header reveals: 'Werkzeug/3.0.6 Python/3.8.17', exposing both the WSGI framework version and the Python interpreter version. This information helps attackers identify known vulnerabilities specific to these versions and target their attacks accordingly. Python 3.8.17 is also an older, potentially unmaintained version.
steps: 1. Send any HTTP request to the server: curl -s -I http://blog:5003/. 2. Observe the response header: 'Server: Werkzeug/3.0.6 Python/3.8.17'. 3. This reveals the exact versions of Werkzeug and Python in use, enabling version-specific vulnerability research.

title: Session Cookie Missing 'Secure' and 'SameSite' Attributes
description: The application's session cookie is issued without the 'Secure' flag and without a 'SameSite' attribute. The absence of the Secure flag means the cookie can be transmitted over unencrypted HTTP connections, exposing it to network interception (MITM attacks). The absence of SameSite attribute (or SameSite=None) means the cookie is sent in all cross-origin requests, enabling CSRF attacks. The cookie is: session=...; HttpOnly; Path=/ — no Secure, no SameSite.
steps: 1. Log in and capture the Set-Cookie header: curl -s -X POST http://blog:5003/login --data-urlencode 'username=testuser' --data-urlencode 'password=Test1234!' -D -. 2. Observe the Set-Cookie header: 'session=<token>; HttpOnly; Path=/'. 3. Note the absence of 'Secure' (allows HTTP transmission) and 'SameSite' (enables CSRF). The SameSite omission is also what makes the CSRF attack possible.

title: Hardcoded administrator credentials enabling full admin compromise
description: The application contains hardcoded administrator credentials, superhacker:s3cureP@ssw0rd. These credentials allow direct login to the system as an admin user and result in issuance of a valid JWT token with administrative privileges, enabling complete application compromise.
steps:
1. Send a login request to the authentication endpoint.
2. Use the credentials superhacker / s3cureP@ssw0rd.
3. Example:
   curl -X POST http://finance:8000/auth/login -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"username":"superhacker","password":"s3cureP@ssw0rd"}'
4. Observe successful authentication and receipt of an admin JWT token with role admin.

title: Missing security headers
description: HTTP responses are missing important security headers, including X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, Strict-Transport-Security, and Content-Security-Policy. The absence of these headers reduces browser-side protections and increases exposure to attacks such as clickjacking and MIME-type confusion.
steps:
1. Send a request to the application, for example:
   curl -I http://finance:8000/
2. Inspect the returned headers.
3. Observe that headers such as X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, Strict-Transport-Security, and Content-Security-Policy are missing.

title: Excessive API Endpoint Exposure Without Proper Access Controls
description: Numerous sensitive API endpoints are exposed without adequate authentication or authorization requirements, expanding the attack surface and creating potential unauthorized access paths. Reported examples include GET /bank/transactions/history?account={account}, GET /bank/loan/user?userID={userID}, POST /auth/register, POST /user/reset-password, POST /user/delete-account, POST /bank/account, and POST /bank/get-credit-card.
steps: Enumerate and directly access sensitive API endpoints and verify that functionality or information is exposed without sufficient access controls. Examples include /bank/transactions/history, /bank/loan/user, /user/reset-password, /user/delete-account, /bank/account, and /bank/get-credit-card.

title: Multiple SQL Injection Points Across Critical Endpoints
description: Several application endpoints were reported as vulnerable to SQL injection. The source reports differ on the exact affected endpoints: report.md mentions registration, login, and financial endpoints, with examples including POST /auth/register and GET /bank/transactions/history?account={injection}; final_report identifies POST /auth/login, POST /bank/loan/request, and POST /bank/get-credit-card. Testing included error-based, UNION-based, and time-based payloads, with reported impact including database structure exposure and potential data extraction.
steps: Submit SQL injection payloads to reported parameters, such as POST /auth/register, POST /auth/login, GET /bank/transactions/history?account=<payload>, POST /bank/loan/request, or POST /bank/get-credit-card, depending on the tested endpoint. Use payloads like test', UNION SELECT, or time-based payloads and observe SQL errors or other injection indicators.

title: Missing Security Headers
description: The frontend and backend responses are missing multiple important HTTP security headers, including `Content-Security-Policy`, `X-Content-Type-Options`, `X-Frame-Options`, and `Strict-Transport-Security`. The final report also notes missing `Referrer-Policy` and `Permissions-Policy`. Their absence increases exposure to XSS, clickjacking, MIME-type confusion, transport security downgrade risks, and browser data leakage.
steps:
1. Browse to `http://finance:8000` or inspect responses from the backend API.
2. Open browser Developer Tools or intercept the response.
3. Review the response headers.
4. Observe that headers such as `Content-Security-Policy`, `X-Content-Type-Options`, `X-Frame-Options`, `Strict-Transport-Security`, `Referrer-Policy`, and `Permissions-Policy` are absent.

title: Exposed Werkzeug Debug Console
description: The Werkzeug debug console is exposed at `/console` without authentication. The report identifies this as an unauthenticated remote code execution endpoint, with PIN authentication required to unlock the console.
steps: Browse to `http://bank:5000/console` and observe the exposed Werkzeug debugger console and PIN prompt.

title: Virtual Card Management Broken SQL Functionality
description: The virtual card management feature has broken SQL functionality revealing implementation flaws.
steps: Interact with the virtual card management functionality and observe SQL-related failures or broken behavior that reveal implementation flaws.

title: Development Server in Production
description: The application is running with the Flask development server in a production environment with debug mode enabled.
steps: Inspect the application behavior or response headers and confirm it is running on the Flask/Werkzeug development server with debug mode enabled.

title: JWT token exposure in session management
description: The application uses JWT tokens for authentication and returns them in responses and session cookies after login. The report highlights this as a security concern due to potential token manipulation or session hijacking risk if token handling, validation, or key management is weak, although no concrete validation flaw was demonstrated during testing.
steps: Send a POST request to /login with valid credentials and observe that the response includes a JWT token in the response body and a session cookie. Review token handling and validation controls.

title: Sensitive financial data exposure via API
description: The /api/v1/accounts endpoint exposes sensitive financial information in API responses. The documented example includes account_number, balance, and card_number values returned in clear form, indicating insufficient masking or access restriction for financial data.
steps: Send a GET request to /api/v1/accounts and inspect the JSON response for exposed financial fields such as account_number, balance, and card_number.

title: Werkzeug debug console exposure
description: The application exposes the Werkzeug debug console at /console. The page is reachable in production and discloses debugger metadata in the HTML/JavaScript, including CONSOLE_MODE, EVALEX, and a SECRET value. The console is still PIN-protected, so the documented impact is potential remote code execution if the PIN is compromised.
steps: Send a GET request to /console and verify that the Werkzeug console page is returned. Inspect the response source for debugger variables such as CONSOLE_MODE, EVALEX, and the exposed SECRET value, and note that PIN authentication is still required.

title: Missing security headers
description: The application omits important HTTP security headers. The reports explicitly note missing X-Frame-Options and X-Content-Type-Options headers, and the detailed final report also identifies a missing Content-Security-Policy header. This weakens browser-side protections and can enable clickjacking, MIME confusion, and reduced protection against client-side injection attacks.
steps:
1. Send an HTTP request to the application, such as to http://blog:5003/.
2. Inspect the HTTP response headers.
3. Verify that X-Frame-Options is not present.
4. Verify that X-Content-Type-Options is not present.
5. The detailed final report additionally notes that Content-Security-Policy is not present.

title: Server information disclosure
description: The application discloses detailed server and runtime version information in HTTP responses via the Server header, including "Werkzeug/3.0.6 Python/3.8.17". This can help attackers tailor exploits and research version-specific weaknesses. Severity is inconsistent across the supplied materials: report.md lists this issue as LOW, while the detailed final_report rates it as MEDIUM with CVSS 5.3. This finding follows the detailed final_report severity while noting the discrepancy.
steps:
1. Send any HTTP request to the application.
2. Inspect the HTTP response headers.
3. Observe the Server header disclosing version details, for example: Server: Werkzeug/3.0.6 Python/3.8.17.

title: Open redirect via unvalidated `next` parameter
description: The application accepts an unvalidated `next` parameter and allows redirection to arbitrary external domains. The primary report identifies this on `/logout` and `/create_draft`, while the final report also describes the same issue on `/login`. This enables phishing and malicious redirection attacks.
steps:
1. Navigate to a URL using the vulnerable parameter, for example `http://blog:5003/logout?next=http://evil.com`, `http://blog:5003/create_draft?next=http://evil.com`, or `http://blog:5003/login?next=http://evil.com`
2. Trigger the relevant workflow
3. Observe that the application redirects the browser to the external domain
4. Optionally test encoded payloads such as `http%3A%2F%2Fevil.com`

title: Server version information exposure
description: The application exposes server and runtime version information in HTTP headers, including Werkzeug/3.0.6 and Python/3.8.17. This assists attacker reconnaissance and version-targeted exploitation.
steps:
1. Send a request such as `curl -I http://blog:5003`
2. Inspect the response headers
3. Observe exposed values such as `Server: Werkzeug/3.0.6` and `X-Powered-By: Python/3.8.17`

title: Missing Subresource Integrity for external Bootstrap CDN resource
description: The application loads Bootstrap 4.0.0 from an external CDN without `integrity` and `crossorigin` attributes. This increases risk from CDN compromise or resource tampering.
steps:
1. View the page source of the application
2. Locate the Bootstrap stylesheet reference to `https://maxcdn.bootstrapcdn.com/bootstrap/4.0.0/css/bootstrap.min.css`
3. Verify that the tag lacks `integrity` and `crossorigin` attributes

title: SQL Injection in registration username parameter
description: The username parameter in the registration endpoint is vulnerable to SQL injection. The report states both boolean-based and UNION-based injection were confirmed, allowing database manipulation, schema extraction, user data exfiltration, and possible authentication bypass.
steps:
1. Navigate to http://blog:5003/register
2. Enter payload `admin' OR '1'='1` in the username field
3. Enter any password and email
4. Submit the form and observe a 302 redirect response indicating successful injection
5. Test additional payloads such as `admin' AND 1=1`, `admin' AND 1=2`, and `admin' UNION SELECT NULL--`

title: Session cookie security issues
description: Session cookies lack both the Secure flag and SameSite attribute, as reported together in the source as a single session management issue. This weakens protection against interception and cross-site request abuse, and the final report notes associated risks including cookie theft via MITM attacks and facilitation of CSRF attacks.
steps:
1. Log in to the application
2. Inspect cookies in browser developer tools
3. Locate the session cookie
4. Verify that the `Secure` attribute is not set
5. Verify that the `SameSite` attribute is not set

title: Missing security headers
description: The application is missing multiple recommended security headers across responses, increasing exposure to client-side attacks. Reported missing headers include Content-Security-Policy, X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, Strict-Transport-Security, and also X-XSS-Protection in the final report.
steps:
1. Request an application page such as `http://blog:5003/login`
2. Inspect response headers using browser developer tools or `curl -I http://blog:5003/login`
3. Verify the absence of headers such as `Content-Security-Policy`, `X-Content-Type-Options`, `X-Frame-Options`, and `Strict-Transport-Security`

title: Missing Security Headers
description: The application lacks key security headers, specifically X-Frame-Options and X-Content-Type-Options. This increases exposure to clickjacking and MIME-sniffing attacks. HTTP response inspection showed only basic headers without these protections. The detailed report additionally notes that Strict-Transport-Security was not present, but the consistently supported core issue across both reports is the absence of X-Frame-Options and X-Content-Type-Options.
steps:
1. Send a request to an application endpoint, for example with curl -I http://blog:5003/ or by using browser developer tools.
2. Inspect the response headers.
3. Observe that X-Frame-Options and X-Content-Type-Options are not present.

title: Weak input validation/type confusion on banking endpoints
description: Banking endpoints accept string numerics for parameters expected to be identifiers and amounts, and handle malformed types inconsistently. Although no standalone injection or auth bypass was confirmed, this weak validation lowers security assurances and supports abuse of the authorization flaws.
steps:
1. Send POST /bank/account with a body such as {"userID":"2"}.
2. Send POST /bank/loan/request with a body such as {"userID":"2","amount":"100"}.
3. Observe that the API accepts and processes string values.
4. Send malformed types such as arrays, objects, floats, or negatives and observe inconsistent error handling instead of strict schema rejection.

title: Password-reset flow at `/user/reset-password` exposes OTP validation behavior
description: The password reset endpoint `http://api.finance:3000/user/reset-password` is externally reachable and returns specific feedback when an incorrect OTP is supplied, such as `{"error":"Invalid OTP"}`. This confirms the reset workflow is exposed and provides behavioral feedback useful for reset-flow probing, but the assessment did not prove password reset compromise or bypass.
steps:
1. Send `POST http://api.finance:3000/user/reset-password`.
2. Use body `{"phoneNumber":"0000000000","otp":"000000","newPassword":"Newpass1!"}`.
3. Observe the response `401 {"error":"Invalid OTP"}`.

title: Unsafe client-side token storage in `localStorage`
description: The SPA stores bearer tokens and `userID` values in `localStorage` and derives authentication state from the token's presence. This makes authentication material accessible to browser-side script execution and increases the impact of any future client-side injection issue.
steps:
1. Download `GET http://finance:8000/static/js/bundle.js.map`.
2. Review the authentication source.
3. Confirm use of `localStorage.setItem('token', ...)` and `localStorage.setItem('userID', ...)`.

title: Weak validation on live delete route
description: The live delete route accepts authenticated deletion requests with no meaningful validation. Requests succeeded both without a body and with irrelevant JSON values. Arbitrary third-party deletion was not confirmed, but the route’s validation is clearly weak.
steps:
1. Authenticate as a test user and obtain a JWT.
2. Send:
   curl -i -X DELETE http://api.finance:3000/user/detele-account \
     -H 'Authorization: Bearer <valid_jwt>'
3. Observe 200 success.
4. Repeat with an irrelevant JSON body and observe the same result.

title: Authentication and token-handling observations
description: The API uses Bearer JWT authentication in the Authorization header. No Set-Cookie or server-managed session behavior was observed. JWTs included userID, username, iat, and exp claims, with an observed lifetime of approximately 3600 seconds and HS256 signing.
steps:
1. Log in with valid credentials.
2. Observe response JSON containing token and userID.
3. Use the token in Authorization: Bearer <token> for protected routes.
4. Note absence of Set-Cookie in responses.
5. Decode the JWT and observe claims including userID, username, iat, and exp, along with an approximate 3600-second lifetime and HS256 signing.

title: Duplicate username handling is inconsistent
description: The system accepted registration of a new user with username superhacker despite that username already being in use by the original admin account. This indicates broken or inconsistent uniqueness enforcement, which can create identity confusion and downstream authorization problems.
steps:
1. Register a user with an existing username known to be present.
2. Observe successful 201 creation instead of a uniqueness error.

title: Missing common security hardening headers on frontend
description: Common browser hardening headers were not observed on the frontend responses. Missing headers included Content-Security-Policy, Strict-Transport-Security, X-Frame-Options, and X-Content-Type-Options.
steps:
1. Request the frontend root or representative static responses:
   curl -i http://finance:8000/
2. Inspect response headers.
3. Confirm the absence of CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options, and X-Content-Type-Options.

title: Swagger UI and OpenAPI documentation exposed
description: The backend exposes Swagger UI and an embedded OpenAPI spec, which materially assists route discovery and attack-surface mapping. This is not inherently a vulnerability but is highly useful to attackers when combined with other weaknesses.
steps:
1. Request:
   curl -i http://api.finance:3000/api-docs
2. Follow redirect to /api-docs/.
3. Retrieve /api-docs/swagger-ui-init.js and inspect documented routes and security schemes.

title: Route mismatch between documented DELETE /user/account and live DELETE /user/detele-account
description: The documented delete endpoint and the implemented delete endpoint do not match. Swagger references DELETE /user/account, but the live implementation is DELETE /user/detele-account. This inconsistency can cause operational mistakes, documentation drift, and hidden functionality.
steps:
1. Send:
   curl -i -X DELETE http://api.finance:3000/user/account
2. Observe 404 Cannot DELETE /user/account.
3. Send:
   curl -i -X DELETE http://api.finance:3000/user/detele-account \
     -H 'Authorization: Bearer <valid_jwt>'
4. Observe 200 {"message":"Account deleted successfully"}.

title: Profile upload permits traversal-style filenames without proving directory escape
description: The upload handler accepts traversal-like filenames such as ../traversed.txt and stores them after normalization inside the uploads directory. While escape outside the upload directory was not confirmed, the acceptance of traversal-style input indicates weak filename handling.
steps: Authenticate as a normal user and POST /upload_profile_picture with multipart filename ../traversed.txt. Observe 200 OK with a normalized file_path under static/uploads and successful retrieval of the file from that location.

title: Exposed Werkzeug debugger leaks internal debugger secret and confirms debug console presence
description: The Werkzeug interactive debugger is exposed at /console. The page discloses debugger state variables including EVALEX status and a secret token used by debugger functionality. This is a sensitive information disclosure and a strong indicator of unsafe production debug exposure.
steps: Anonymous: GET /console and observe 200 OK page 'Console // Werkzeug Debugger' with variables such as CONSOLE_MODE=true, EVALEX=true, EVALEX_TRUSTED=false, TRACEBACK=-1, and SECRET="iMhcnYM3sXTcaRuZel60". Also GET /console?__debugger__=yes&cmd=resource&f=debugger.js and observe 200 OK confirming active debugger resources.

title: AI anonymous chat endpoint discloses implementation details and unsafe behavior
description: The anonymous AI chat endpoint is accessible without authentication, identifies the backend provider and model, and responds in a way that indicates unsafe instruction handling. Rate-limit responses also disclose internal IP information and policy metadata.
steps: Anonymous: POST /api/ai/chat/anonymous with JSON {"message":"test"} and observe 200 OK with metadata such as api_used, model, mode anonymous, and warning text. Repeatedly call the endpoint until rate limited and observe 429 response leaking internal client_ip, current_count, limit, limit_type, and window_hours.

title: Login and registration forms use incorrect method while form-encoded POST causes server error
description: The HTML forms at /login and /register use GET, but the backend only works with JSON POST. Form-encoded POST requests trigger HTTP 500 errors with parsing-related details. This creates broken authentication UX and leaks server-side error information.
steps: Anonymous: GET /login and GET /register and observe HTML forms use method=get. Then POST form-encoded data to /login or /register and observe 500 errors such as 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'get'.

title: Excessive Debug Exposure in Login Response
description: The login endpoint returned internal metadata beyond what was required for authentication. Successful responses included accountNumber, isAdmin, and debug_info.user_id in addition to the JWT, increasing exposure of internal identity metadata.
steps:
1. Send a POST request to http://bank:5000/login with valid credentials.
2. Observe a 200 OK response.
3. Confirm the response includes token plus additional fields such as accountNumber, isAdmin, and debug_info.user_id.

title: Inconsistent Authentication Enforcement on /dashboard
description: The /dashboard route showed inconsistent authentication behavior. It was observed returning 401 JSON without authentication in some cases, but at other times returned full dashboard HTML without an Authorization header. Invalid bearer tokens reliably produced 401, while valid tokens returned dashboard content. This indicates flawed or inconsistent access-control enforcement.
steps:
1. Send GET requests to http://bank:5000/dashboard without an Authorization header multiple times.
2. Observe that responses may vary between 401 JSON and 200 HTML.
3. Send GET /dashboard with Authorization: Bearer invalidtoken and observe 401 Invalid token.
4. Send GET /dashboard with a valid JWT and observe 200 dashboard HTML.

title: Bill payment card mode accepts foreign, missing, or nonexistent card IDs
description: The bill payment creation endpoint accepts payment_method set to card even when the provided card_id belongs to another user, is omitted, or refers to a nonexistent card. Successful responses and resulting history entries show no valid card linkage, allowing fraudulent card-backed payment records.
steps:
1. Authenticate as a normal user.
2. Send POST /api/bill-payments/create with payment_method set to "card" and a foreign card_id.
3. Observe 200 OK and successful payment processing.
4. Repeat with no card_id and with a nonexistent card_id.
5. Observe that both are also accepted.
6. Request GET /api/bill-payments/history and confirm pending card payments are recorded with null card linkage.

title: Exposed Werkzeug debugger interface
description: A live Werkzeug debugger interface is publicly reachable at /console. Although it was locked by PIN during testing, the page exposes debugger state, secrets, and interactive console indicators. Public exposure of this interface is high risk and can lead to severe compromise if the PIN or trust conditions are bypassed.
steps:
1. Request GET /console.
2. Observe 200 OK and a Werkzeug debugger page showing details such as Console Locked, EVALEX=true, and a leaked SECRET value.
3. Request /console?__debugger__=yes&cmd=resource&f=debugger.js and observe debugger assets are accessible.
4. Optionally request the pinauth endpoint and observe that the authentication flow is exposed.

title: Session cookie lacks SameSite protection
description: The application issues the session cookie without a SameSite attribute, increasing exposure to cross-site request contexts and worsening CSRF exploitability.
steps:
1. Trigger session issuance, for example via successful POST /login.
2. Inspect the Set-Cookie header.
3. Verify the session cookie includes HttpOnly and Path but no SameSite attribute.

title: Session cookie lacks Secure attribute
description: The application issues the session cookie without the Secure flag. If the application is reachable over plaintext HTTP, the cookie may be transmitted without TLS, increasing session theft risk.
steps:
1. Trigger session issuance, such as by POST /login or an unauthenticated request that sets a session cookie.
2. Inspect the Set-Cookie header.
3. Verify the session cookie does not include the Secure attribute.

title: Missing Security Headers (`Content-Security-Policy`, `X-Frame-Options`, `X-Content-Type-Options`, `Strict-Transport-Security`)
description: Multiple application responses were missing common security hardening headers: `Content-Security-Policy`, `X-Frame-Options`, `X-Content-Type-Options`, and `Strict-Transport-Security`. Their absence reduces defense-in-depth against script/content injection, clickjacking, MIME sniffing, and HTTPS enforcement on future visits.
steps:
1. Request representative pages such as `GET /`, `GET /login`, `GET /create_draft`, and `GET /edit_draft/4`.
2. Review the response headers.
3. Confirm that `Content-Security-Policy`, `X-Frame-Options`, `X-Content-Type-Options`, and `Strict-Transport-Security` are absent.
4. Repeat on a default error page such as `GET /nope` to confirm the issue is consistent across responses.

title: Insecure Session Cookie Attributes (`Secure` and `SameSite` Absent)
description: The application sets its session cookie without the `Secure` or `SameSite` attributes. Observed login response set `session=...; HttpOnly; Path=/`, and the logout clearing cookie also lacked `Secure` and `SameSite`. Without `Secure`, the cookie is not explicitly restricted to HTTPS transport. Without `SameSite`, browser-level CSRF resistance is weakened, which directly supports the confirmed CSRF issues on draft creation and editing.
steps:
1. Submit a valid login request: `POST /login`.
2. Observe the response header `Set-Cookie: session=...; HttpOnly; Path=/`.
3. Confirm that the cookie lacks `Secure` and `SameSite`.
4. Request `GET /logout`.
5. Observe the clearing cookie header and confirm `Secure` and `SameSite` are absent there as well.

title: Framework and Server Version Disclosure via Headers and Default Error Pages
description: The application discloses its framework and runtime details in the `Server` header and through default framework error page behavior. Observed header: `Server: Werkzeug/3.0.6 Python/3.8.17`. Default 404 pages also used Werkzeug-style content such as `The requested URL was not found on the server.` This improves attacker fingerprinting, narrows exploit research, and aids version-specific attack planning.
steps:
1. Request any normal page, for example `GET /login`.
2. Observe the response header `Server: Werkzeug/3.0.6 Python/3.8.17`.
3. Request a nonexistent route, for example `GET /nope`.
4. Observe the same `Server` header and a default `404 Not Found` page containing framework-identifying text.
5. Confirm that framework/runtime details are consistently exposed across responses.

title: SSRF via `documentUrl` Parameter
description: The `POST /bank/loan/request` endpoint fetches the user-supplied `documentUrl` without restricting protocols or destinations. This SSRF issue was exploited using multiple protocols including `file://`, `http://`, `gopher://`, and `dict://` to access internal services such as Redis and Memcached, read local files like `/etc/passwd`, and access cloud metadata.
steps:
Submit a loan request containing a malicious `documentUrl`.
Example:
```bash
curl -X POST http://api.finance:3000/bank/loan/request \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"documentUrl":"file:///etc/passwd","userId":2}'
```
Additional testing can use internal URLs or alternate protocols such as `gopher://` and `dict://` to interact with internal services.

title: Stored XSS in Support Chat
description: The `POST /support/conversation` endpoint stores user input without sanitization and later serves it verbatim. This allows persistent cross-site scripting against users or support staff viewing the conversation, enabling script execution in their browsers.
steps: Submit a support message containing a script payload, then view the stored conversation. Example payloads: `` `` ``

title: WebSocket Endpoint Not Implemented
description: A WebSocket endpoint was identified only as a dead-code reference in the frontend bundle and is not actually implemented. This was reported as a low-severity issue.
steps: Inspect the frontend JavaScript bundle for references to a WebSocket endpoint, then attempt to use the referenced endpoint and confirm that it is not implemented.

title: Mass Assignment
description: The `POST /auth/register` endpoint accepts privileged fields such as `role`, `is_admin`, and `balance` from user-controlled input. This can allow attackers to create accounts with elevated privileges or manipulated balances.
steps:
Submit a registration request containing privileged fields.
Example payload:
```json
{"username":"hacker","password":"hack","role":"admin","balance":10000}
```
Observe whether the created account receives elevated privileges or an attacker-controlled balance.

title: Unauthenticated Account Deletion
description: The endpoint `DELETE /user/detele-account` is accessible without authentication, allowing attackers to permanently delete user accounts without authorization. This can cause denial of service and irreversible data loss.
steps:
Send an unauthenticated DELETE request to the endpoint.
Example:
```bash
curl -X DELETE http://api.finance:3000/user/detele-account
```
A successful response indicates that the account was deleted.

title: Empty Credential Authentication Bypass
description: The authentication flow permits login with empty credentials, resulting in a complete authentication bypass. An attacker can obtain authenticated access without providing valid username or password values.
steps: Submit the login request with empty username and password fields. Observe that the application issues a valid authenticated response or token despite the missing credentials.

title: Client-Side localStorage Token Storage Exposure
description: Authentication tokens are stored in browser localStorage on the client side. This storage model increases the risk of token theft through malicious scripts, browser compromise, or cross-origin attack chains, and was explicitly identified as part of the JWT bypass to CORS to data exfiltration scenario.
steps: Authenticate to the application and inspect browser localStorage. Verify that the JWT or bearer token is stored there and can be read by client-side JavaScript.

title: Exposed WebSocket Endpoints
description: WebSocket endpoints used for development or application internals are exposed. These endpoints may disclose additional functionality, metadata, or channels not intended for unauthenticated external access.
steps: Enumerate the application for WebSocket connections and inspect accessible socket endpoints. Confirm that WebSocket endpoints are exposed and reachable from the tested environment.

title: Insecure Authentication Token Storage
description: Authentication tokens are stored in plaintext `localStorage`, making them accessible to JavaScript and therefore retrievable through XSS or client-side compromise. The report also notes lack of cookie security properties, weak session validation, no effective expiration enforcement, and token persistence until manual clearance. This facilitates token theft, replay, and client-side manipulation.
steps:
1. Authenticate through `POST http://api.finance:3000/auth/login`.
2. Open the browser console on `http://finance:8000`.
3. Run `localStorage.getItem('token')`.
4. Observe that the JWT is stored in plaintext and can be extracted or exfiltrated by malicious JavaScript.

title: SPA Catch-All Routing Obscurity
description: The React SPA returns `index.html` with HTTP 200 for most non-existent or unexpected paths. While common in SPAs, this obscures distinctions between missing files, invalid routes, and potentially sensitive paths, making error handling and monitoring less clear and masking resource state during reconnaissance.
steps:
1. Request arbitrary invalid paths such as `http://finance:8000/nonexistent-path`.
2. Request paths resembling sensitive resources such as `/.env` or `/.git/HEAD`.
3. Observe that they return HTTP 200 with the SPA `index.html` content instead of 404 or another appropriate error.

title: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) via Frontend Proxy
description: The frontend proxy endpoint `/api/v1/proxy` forwards attacker-supplied URLs without validation or authentication. This enables SSRF to internal hosts and special addresses such as `localhost`, `127.0.0.1`, `0.0.0.0`, `api.finance:3000`, IPv6 loopback, and cloud metadata service `169.254.169.254`. Full response bodies are returned, permitting internal service enumeration and metadata exposure.
steps:
1. Send a request to `GET http://finance:8000/api/v1/proxy?url=http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/`.
2. Observe metadata returned through the proxy.
3. Repeat with internal targets such as `http://localhost:3000`, `http://127.0.0.1`, or `http://api.finance:3000/health`.
4. Confirm the endpoint is unauthenticated and returns internal responses directly.

title: CDN Supply Chain Risk
description: The application loads third-party resources from `cdn.jsdelivr.net`, including Bootstrap and Bootstrap Icons, without Subresource Integrity protection. Although not an exploitable vulnerability by itself, this introduces supply chain risk if the CDN or referenced assets are compromised or altered.
steps:
1. Inspect the HTML returned by `http://finance:8000/` or monitor network requests.
2. Identify external dependencies loaded from `cdn.jsdelivr.net`.
3. Confirm SRI hashes are not present on the related resource tags.

title: Path Traversal Bypass
description: File-serving paths accept traversal-style input that bypasses initial filtering, including encoded and double-encoding style patterns such as `....//`. Although the current behavior returns the SPA fallback page rather than raw file contents, the bypass demonstrates inadequate path normalization and input validation that could become exploitable under different routing or deployment conditions.
steps:
1. Request traversal paths such as `http://finance:8000/static/js/../../../etc/passwd`.
2. Try encoded variants like `%2e%2e/%2e%2e/%2e%2e/etc/passwd` and bypass forms like `....//....//....//etc/passwd`.
3. Observe that traversal patterns are accepted past initial checks but ultimately return SPA fallback HTML instead of the requested file.

title: Unrestricted HTTP Methods on /admin/ Endpoint
description: The `/admin/` endpoint accepts a wide range of HTTP methods including GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, PATCH, and HEAD, generally returning HTTP 200, with OPTIONS returning 204. There is no proper method restriction or demonstrated access control enforcement. This expands the attack surface and may expose unintended administrative behavior.
steps:
1. Send requests using different HTTP methods to `http://finance:8000/admin/`, including GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, PATCH, HEAD, and OPTIONS.
2. Observe that unsupported methods are not rejected with HTTP 405.
3. Confirm most methods return HTTP 200 and OPTIONS returns 204.

title: Client-Side Only Route Guards
description: Protected frontend routes rely only on a client-side check of `!!localStorage.getItem('token')`. Any truthy value in `localStorage` grants access to routes such as `/update-profile`, `/update-password`, `/delete-account`, `/main`, `/profile`, and `/transactions`, without meaningful server-side validation before rendering. This allows easy bypass of UI-level access restrictions.
steps:
1. In the browser console, run `localStorage.setItem('token', 'fake_token_value')`.
2. Navigate to a protected route such as `http://finance:8000/update-profile`.
3. Observe that the route is accessible because the client only checks whether a token value exists.
4. Replace the token with an empty string and note that access is denied, confirming the guard is purely truthiness-based.

title: CSRF Vulnerability on State-Changing Banking Endpoints
description: State-changing endpoints do not implement CSRF protections. The application lacks CSRF token validation and does not validate `Origin` or `Referer`; the report also notes absent SameSite protections. As a result, a malicious page can trigger unauthorized actions such as account creation, loan requests, profile updates, or fund transfers on behalf of an authenticated victim.
steps:
1. Create a malicious HTML form targeting a state-changing endpoint such as `POST http://api.finance:3000/payment/transfer`.
2. Populate hidden fields with attacker-chosen transfer details.
3. Host the page on an attacker-controlled domain.
4. Induce an authenticated victim to visit the page.
5. The form auto-submits and the backend processes the request successfully without any CSRF token or origin validation.

title: X-Powered-By Header Exposure
description: Responses expose technology stack information via the `X-Powered-By: Express` header, alongside additional server identification such as `WSGIServer/0.2 CPython/3.11.4`. This disclosure assists attackers in fingerprinting the environment and selecting framework- or version-specific attack paths.
steps:
1. Send a request such as `curl -I http://finance:8000/` or to any API endpoint.
2. Inspect response headers.
3. Observe `X-Powered-By: Express` and other server identification values in the response.

title: Missing Security Headers
description: The application lacks multiple standard security headers across tested endpoints, including `X-Frame-Options`, `Content-Security-Policy`, `X-Content-Type-Options`, `Strict-Transport-Security`, and `X-XSS-Protection`. This weakens browser-side defenses and increases exposure to attacks such as clickjacking, MIME sniffing, and XSS exploitation.
steps:
1. Request endpoints such as `/`, `/login`, `/sup3r_s3cr3t_admin`, or `/check_balance/1`.
2. Inspect the HTTP response headers.
3. Observe that the documented security headers are missing.

title: Reflected Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)
description: The `/login` endpoint reflects user-supplied input from the username field without proper output encoding, enabling reflected XSS. The report confirms successful reflection of payloads including ``, ``, and `
steps:
1. Send a `POST /login` request with JSON content.
2. Set the `username` field to a payload such as `<script>alert(1)</script>`.
3. Submit any password value.
4. Observe that the payload is reflected in the response without proper sanitization.

title: Werkzeug Debug Console Exposure
description: The Werkzeug interactive debugger is exposed at `/console` in production with debug-related settings such as `EVALEX=true` and `CONSOLE_MODE=true`. The HTML source exposes secret material and debugger configuration details. The console is PIN-protected, and during testing PIN generation attempts were unsuccessful and the console became locked, but the exposed debugger still constitutes a critical security misconfiguration.
steps:
1. Browse to `/console` on the target application.
2. Observe that the Werkzeug debugger interface is exposed in production.
3. Inspect the page source or response content and note exposed debugger configuration and secret material.
4. Observe that the console is PIN-protected and was locked during testing.

title: Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI)
description: The `/transfer` endpoint is vulnerable to Jinja2 server-side template injection through the `description` parameter. User-controlled input is evaluated server-side, allowing expression evaluation and potentially arbitrary Python code execution.
steps:
1. Authenticate and obtain a valid JWT.
2. Send a `POST /transfer` request with `"description":"{{7*7}}"`.
3. Observe that the response evaluates the expression and returns `49`.
4. Replace the test payload with a Jinja2 code-execution payload to confirm deeper server-side execution impact.

title: Session Fixation in Authentication Flow
description: The report lists Session Fixation as a finding in this banking application. In the application's authentication flow, session state is not handled securely enough during login or privilege changes, allowing a pre-established session context to persist instead of being rotated after authentication.
steps: 1. Establish a session with the application before login. 2. Authenticate to the application using that existing session context. 3. Check whether the session is regenerated after login. 4. Observe that the pre-authentication session remains valid, consistent with the session fixation finding reported.

title: Werkzeug Debugger RCE
description: The application exposes the Werkzeug interactive debugger at GET /console while running in debug mode. The report states the debugger SECRET was hardcoded in the page source (RRENWh8od68Y6sl5tTRh) and valid PIN candidates were calculated, enabling remote code execution after restart. Practical exploitation during testing was blocked only by an exhausted in-memory attempt counter.
steps: 1. Request GET /console. 2. Extract the hardcoded SECRET from the HTML source. 3. Calculate candidate debugger PINs using the documented host and application values. 4. After service restart or with available attempts, authenticate to the debugger with a calculated PIN. 5. Use the debugger eval functionality to execute arbitrary Python code.

title: Server Version Information Disclosure via Server Header
description: HTTP responses disclose exact server and runtime versions via the Server header. The report specifically identifies disclosure of Werkzeug/2.0.1 and Python/3.9.25, which assists attackers in fingerprinting the application stack.
steps: 1. Send a request such as HEAD /login. 2. Inspect the response headers. 3. Observe the Server header disclosing Werkzeug/2.0.1 Python/3.9.25.

title: Registration Schema Bypass
description: The report lists Registration Schema Bypass as a finding. The supplied detailed report indicates the registration flow at /register triggers a schema mismatch involving an expected email field, showing the registration process is broken due to improper schema handling.
steps: 1. Send a POST request to /register with registration data including username, password, and email. 2. Observe that the registration flow does not handle the schema correctly and returns an error related to the expected email field. 3. Use this behavior to confirm the registration schema bypass condition described in the report.

title: Missing Security Headers
description: The application does not set important HTTP security headers such as X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, Content-Security-Policy, Strict-Transport-Security, and Referrer-Policy. This weakens browser-side protections and reduces defense in depth against common web attacks.
steps:
1. Send a request to the application, for example to /.
2. Inspect the HTTP response headers.
3. Verify that recommended security headers like CSP, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, HSTS, and Referrer-Policy are missing.

title: Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI) Payload Storage
description: User-controlled input accepts Jinja2-style template expressions such as {{7*7}} and stores them in the application. During testing the payloads were rendered as plaintext rather than executed, indicating mitigation in the current rendering path, but the application still accepts dangerous template syntax and could become exploitable if rendering behavior changes.
steps:
1. Submit a Jinja2-style payload such as {{7*7}} or {{config.SECRET_KEY}} through an input field like the transfer description.
2. Store the payload in the application.
3. Access a page that displays the stored content, such as the admin panel.
4. Confirm the template syntax is accepted and persisted, even if currently displayed as literal text.

title: Insecure session cookie configuration
description: The Flask session cookie is missing important security attributes. Although `HttpOnly` is present, the cookie does not set `Secure` or `SameSite`, which increases the risk of session exposure over unencrypted transport and allows cookies to be included in cross-site requests, supporting CSRF scenarios. The report also notes that signed session cookie contents expose internal application state such as flash-message data.
steps:
1. Authenticate to the application and capture the `Set-Cookie` response header.
2. Inspect the session cookie attributes.
3. Confirm that `HttpOnly` is present but `Secure` and `SameSite` are absent.
4. Verify the cookie is transmitted over plain HTTP requests.
5. Optionally decode the Flask session cookie payload to observe stored client-side session data.

title: Server version information disclosure
description: The application discloses detailed version information in the `Server` header, including `Werkzeug/3.0.6` and `Python/3.8.17`. This reveals exact framework and runtime versions to attackers and aids reconnaissance and targeted exploitation. The disclosed Python version is also noted as end-of-life as of October 2024.
steps:
1. Send a request such as `HEAD /login`.
2. Inspect the `Server` response header.
3. Confirm that exact framework and runtime versions are disclosed, e.g. `Werkzeug/3.0.6 Python/3.8.17`.
4. Repeat on additional endpoints to verify consistent disclosure.

title: Missing HTTP security headers
description: HTTP responses are missing multiple security headers, including `X-Frame-Options`, `Content-Security-Policy`, `Strict-Transport-Security`, and `Referrer-Policy`. The final report also notes the absence of `X-Content-Type-Options` and `Permissions-Policy`. These omissions reduce browser-enforced protections against attacks such as clickjacking, MIME sniffing, XSS impact amplification, SSL stripping, and referrer leakage.
steps:
1. Send a HEAD or GET request to an application endpoint such as `/login`.
2. Review the response headers.
3. Confirm that headers such as `X-Frame-Options`, `Content-Security-Policy`, `Strict-Transport-Security`, `Referrer-Policy`, and related security headers are absent.
4. Repeat on other endpoints to verify the issue is application-wide.

title: Robots.txt Information Disclosure
description: The robots.txt file discloses sensitive application paths such as /dev, /debug, /internal, and /admin. Although intended for search engine guidance, it provides attackers with reconnaissance data about hidden or sensitive endpoints.
steps: Request http://blog:5003/robots.txt and review the listed Disallow entries. Use the disclosed paths such as /dev, /debug, /internal, or /admin to identify sensitive application areas.

title: Application Debug Information Exposure via /debug
description: The /debug endpoint exposes application debug information including Python version, Werkzeug debug status, secret key length, and database URI. This is distinct from the broader 'Debug Mode Enabled in Production' misconfiguration: this finding is specifically about the reachable /debug endpoint leaking sensitive internal information. Although report.md summarized debugger exposure as critical, the detailed final_report rates this endpoint exposure as high.
steps: Request http://blog:5003/debug and review the returned debug details. Observe exposed information such as Python version, Werkzeug debug status, secret key length, and database URI path.

title: Weak Registration Input Validation
description: The registration endpoint accepts invalid or unsafe input, including empty usernames, empty passwords, and duplicate usernames, without adequate server-side validation. This can lead to account integrity issues and trivial account creation weaknesses.
steps: Submit POST requests to /register with an empty username, an empty password, or a username that already exists, while including the CSRF token. Verify that the requests are accepted and the resulting accounts appear in user listings or otherwise succeed.

title: Sensitive Information Disclosure via /env
description: The /env endpoint exposes sensitive environment variables including SECRET_KEY, API keys, database connection details, SMTP configuration, and administrative contact information. The final_report rates this issue as critical because the exposed secrets can enable further compromise such as session manipulation, credential abuse, and infrastructure targeting.
steps: Request http://blog:5003/env and inspect the response for entries such as SECRET_KEY, API_KEY, DATABASE_URL, SMTP settings, and ADMIN_EMAIL.

title: Debug Mode Enabled in Production
description: The Flask application is running with debug mode enabled in production. This is distinct from the /debug endpoint exposure: this finding covers the insecure production configuration that can expose stack traces, internal application state, and debugger-related functionality intended only for development, significantly increasing the risk of code execution and information leakage.
steps: Check /debug and response headers to confirm debug-related information, such as Werkzeug and Python details. Trigger error conditions or probe debugger-related paths like /console or /debug/console to confirm that debug mode is enabled and exposing internal behavior.

title: Parameter Tampering / Privilege Escalation via role manipulation
description: The registration workflow accepts a client-supplied role parameter and processes it without proper server-side enforcement. Attackers can submit elevated role values such as admin or super_admin during registration, indicating broken access control and privilege escalation risk. The source reports conflict on severity: report.md lists this as high, while the more detailed final_report rates it as medium. This extraction aligns to the detailed final_report severity.
steps: Submit a POST request to /register including username, password, and a role parameter such as role=admin or role=super_admin along with the CSRF token. Verify the created account and test access to privileged functionality or role-associated resources.

title: Unauthenticated SQL Injection via /dev/run
description: The /dev/run endpoint accepts a query parameter and executes arbitrary SQL directly against the SQLite database without authentication or authorization. This allows full database compromise, including extraction of users, posts, comments, sessions, logs, and schema information.
steps: Send a POST request to /dev/run with a SQL query in the query parameter, for example: curl -X POST http://blog:5003/dev/run -d "query=SELECT id, username, email, role, password FROM users". Repeat with queries against sessions, posts, comments, logs, or PRAGMA statements to enumerate the schema.

title: Python Version Information Disclosure
description: The application exposes its Python version in HTTP response headers, for example via a Server header such as Werkzeug/2.3.4 Python/3.10.11. This assists attackers in identifying the runtime and targeting version-specific vulnerabilities or framework weaknesses.
steps: Send a request for response headers, for example: curl -I http://blog:5003/. Observe that the Server header discloses the Python version and related framework information.

title: Missing Security Headers
description: The application does not return important security headers such as Content-Security-Policy, Strict-Transport-Security, X-Frame-Options, and X-Content-Type-Options. This weakens browser-side protections against clickjacking, MIME sniffing, XSS, and protocol downgrade issues.
steps: Send a HEAD or GET request to the application root, for example curl -I http://blog:5003/, and inspect the response headers. Confirm that headers such as CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, Referrer-Policy, and related protections are absent.

title: HTTP Parameter Pollution (HPP)
description: The application processes duplicate request parameters inconsistently across endpoints. It commonly uses the first occurrence of a repeated parameter, creating opportunities for validation bypasses, authentication confusion, and other unexpected behavior when crafted requests include duplicate keys.
steps:
1. Send a login request with duplicate parameters: `curl -s -d "username=admin&username=test&password=testpass123" http://blog:5003/login`.
2. Observe that the application processes the first `username` value.
3. Send a registration request with duplicate password parameters: `curl -s -d "username=test&password=test123&password=other456&confirm_password=test123" http://blog:5003/register`.
4. Observe that the first password value is used.
5. Test edge cases such as altered parameter casing, for example: `curl -s -d "Username=TESTUSER&password=testpass123" http://blog:5003/login`, and note inconsistent behavior.

title: Session Cookie Issued on Failed Registration
description: The application issues a new session cookie even when registration fails validation. Failed registration responses include `Set-Cookie` for a session containing `_fresh: False`, creating unnecessary session state and introducing session-management weaknesses such as session fixation potential and cookie accumulation.
steps:
1. Submit an invalid registration request and inspect response headers:
   `curl -s -D - -d "username=admin'%20OR%201=1--&password=test&confirm_password=test" http://blog:5003/register`
2. Observe a response header similar to `Set-Cookie: session=...` even though registration failed.
3. Decode the session cookie and confirm it contains state such as `{'_fresh': False}`.

title: Trailing Slash Returns 404
description: Application endpoints such as `/login`, `/register`, and `/dashboard` return HTTP 404 when accessed with trailing slashes, while the same routes work without trailing slashes. This creates inconsistent routing behavior and can cause broken links or user confusion.
steps:
Request application endpoints with and without trailing slashes and compare responses, for example:
- `curl -s http://blog:5003/login` returns HTTP 200
- `curl -s http://blog:5003/login/` returns HTTP 404
- `curl -s http://blog:5003/register` returns HTTP 200
- `curl -s http://blog:5003/register/` returns HTTP 404
- `curl -s http://blog:5003/dashboard/` returns HTTP 404

title: Sequential User IDs in Session Cookies
description: Flask session cookies contain predictable sequential `_user_id` values visible when decoded. These identifiers enable user enumeration and can facilitate further attacks such as targeted access-control testing.
steps:
1. Authenticate and capture a session cookie.
2. Decode the cookie using a tool such as:
   `flask-unsign --decode --cookie "$(cat /tmp/user1_session.txt | grep session | cut -f7)" --no-literal-eval`
3. Observe fields such as `_user_id` with sequential values like `1`, `2`, `3`, or `12`.
4. Compare cookies across multiple users to confirm predictability.

title: Missing Security Headers (CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options)
description: Critical browser security headers are missing on the application hosts, including Content-Security-Policy, Strict-Transport-Security, and X-Frame-Options as reported. This increases exposure to XSS, clickjacking, and downgrade-style attacks.
steps: `curl -s -I http://finance:8000` and `curl -s -I http://api.finance:3000` and inspect for missing CSP, HSTS, and X-Frame-Options headers.

title: JWT Stored in localStorage
description: The frontend stores JWTs in `localStorage`, making them accessible to any JavaScript executing in the origin. This significantly increases the impact of XSS because injected scripts can directly steal authentication tokens.
steps: Inspect `bundle.js` or use the application login flow, then verify token access with payloads such as `localStorage.getItem("token")`.

title: Swagger UI Publicly Accessible
description: The Swagger/OpenAPI documentation at `/api-docs/` is accessible without authentication, exposing endpoint inventory, schemas, and request/response formats to attackers.
steps: `curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" http://api.finance:3000/api-docs/`

title: Stored XSS — Support Ticket System
